name: Groq API
what_it_does: Groq's ultra-fast LLM inference API using custom Language Processing Units (LPUs) to serve open-source models (Llama, Mixtral, Gemma) at industry-leading speeds.
use_cases: Real-time conversational agents requiring sub-100ms token generation
High-throughput text processing where latency is critical
Building voice-to-voice AI systems requiring fast transcription + LLM
Agentic loops where LLM inference speed is the bottleneck
Streaming chat applications needing immediate token output
not_for: Teams needing frontier models like GPT-4 or Claude (Groq only hosts open-source)
Image or audio generation (text inference only)
Long context windows (Groq's context limits can be smaller)
best_when: An agent needs the fastest possible open-source LLM inference, especially for latency-sensitive applications or real-time conversation.
avoid_when: You need proprietary frontier models, multimodal capabilities, or model fine-tuning.

auth.notes: Simple bearer token authentication. OpenAI-compatible auth header format. Keys from Groq console. Single key grants access to all models. Extremely easy for agents.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free tier with rate limits; no credit card required for free usage
pricing.paid_tiers: Llama 3 8B ~$0.05/M input tokens, Llama 3 70B ~$0.59/M input tokens
pricing.notes: Extremely competitive pricing, often cheaper than Together AI. Free tier is genuinely usable for development. No credit card required to start.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: OpenAI-compatible error format. Rate limit errors include Retry-After header and quota information. Error messages are clear and actionable. Very agent-friendly.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: LLM responses are non-deterministic by default. Seed parameter available for reproducibility.
known_agent_gotchas: Free tier rate limits (30 req/min) can be hit quickly by autonomous agents
Model selection is limited to what Groq hosts — cannot use arbitrary open-source models
Context windows are smaller than competitors for some models
No function calling on all models — must check model-specific capabilities
Token limits per request can be lower than advertised context window
No streaming callbacks for tool use — must handle complete response
